**NeuroBand AI Powered Emotion Detection and Therapy Wristband**

**Faculty of Engineering, Sciences and Technology**

### Preface

The following report reports about the design, development, and assessment of the Final Year Design Project named **NeuroBand - AI-Powered Emotion Detection and Therapy Wristband**. The project is aimed at solving the increasing issues of mental stress and emotional state with the help of recent technologies of Artificial Intelligence, Internet of Things (IoT), and wearable systems. This report is mainly aimed at providing a systematic and detailed report on the problem being discussed, the approaches taken and the results obtained in the process of this project. The report indicates the real-life use of theoretical knowledge gained during the undergraduate term of Computer Science. It combines the ideas of embedded systems, machine learning, human-computer interaction, and mobile application development in order to address a real-life issue. Reading this report, the reader will understand how the latest technologies can be used to develop smart, user-friendly healthcare solutions that would work in real time and at the same time provide privacy and efficiency. This Final Year Design Project has been a learning experience to us. It was a chance to operate as a team and to manage the timeframes of the project and to cope with technical and practical difficulties. During the project, we developed our problem-solving skills, acquired technical skills, and had practical experience with the design of systems, embedded programming, and the implementation of AI models on resource-constrained devices..

.

### Generative AI and Originality Declaration

The current students, the following, pledge on this statement that the materials presented in our undergraduate project project, that is, **NeuroBand - AI-Powered Emotion Detection and Therapy Wristband**, has all been done by us personally and without any major assistance of another person or even the Generative AI technologies. Any small help or contribution that was obtained in the course of coming up with this work has been duly recognized and mentioned. The entire project was prepared following the current plagiarism policies published by the Higher Education Commission (HEC) and the IQRA University, as well as the institutional provisions regarding the ethical use of the Generative AI tools.

We are well aware of the policy of zero tolerance of HEC and IQRA University towards plagiarism. It is thus our belief that there is no plagiarism in any section of this project and that all materials that were used have been cited appropriately in accordance with the academic norms.

We also commit to the fact that in case any form of plagiarism is discovered in this project at any point, even after the degree is awarded, the University can withdraw or revoke our degree. Also, HEC and the University can post our names on HEC or University site where the names of students who engaged in plagiarism activity are posted.

The project is a culmination of the aforementioned above, and we are truly grateful to all those who had been helpful, guided, or provided assistance in any way, to the successful completion of this project.

##### Have successfully completed their Final Year Project named

**NeuroBand AI Powered Emotion Detection and Therapy Wristband**

**In the partial fulfillment of the Degree of Bachelor of Science in Computer Science**

**Miss Arshee**

Lecturer

### Abstract

In this report, the design and development of NeuroBand - an emotion detection and therapy wristband powered by AI is presented, it is a wearable device expected to monitor stress in real-time and provide immediate therapeutic response. The project helps overcome the increasing problem of mental stress in students and working professionals because it offers an active, self-governing, and privacy-sensitive solution. In contrast to the conventional mental health applications using cloud computing and feedback lag, NeuroBand conducts on-device stress sensors with embedded machine learning.

The main goal of the project is to create the wearable wristband that will be able to constantly track physiological signals and recognize stress in real time. Several biosensors, such as heart rate, galvanic skin response, temperature, and motion sensors, are incorporated into the system to receive pertinent physiologically related data. With the TinyML, lightweight machine learning models are implemented on a low-power microcontroller to classify stress levels in an efficient manner with low latency and power usage.

Upon detection of stress, the system will start providing immediate therapeutic assistance with the available pre-integrated intervention packages like guided breathing exercises with vibration and light patterns, playing calming music, and voice-based chatbot. A companion mobile application is created in order to allow visualization of the trends of stress, history of the therapy, and control of the system with the help of Bluetooth Low Energy communication.

The project shows that it is possible to integrate IoT, embedded Artificial Intelligence, and wearable to develop an efficient mental health support system. The findings show that NeuroBand can detect stress in real-time, intervene on it timely, and interact easily with users besides keeping their data private since it is not dependent on the cloud. The overall finding of this work is that wearable-based AI-powered devices can have a tremendous role in the proactive management of mental health and provide scalable solutions in the future to be applied in healthcare and wellness..

**_Keywords: NeuroBand, Stress Detection, Wearable Technology, TinyML, Embedded Artificial Intelligence, Mental Health, IoT_**

| **Range of conflicting requirements** | **WP2: wide-ranging or Conflicting technical, engineering/computing and other issues** | Involves complex signal processing and emotion recognition using physiological data. |
| **Depth of analysis required** | **WP3: no obvious solution** | Integrates ML, IoT, NLP, and embedded programming domains |
| **Familiarity of issues** | **WP4: Involve**<br><br>**infrequently encountered issues** | Tackles an infrequently explored domain of mental health monitoring on embedded devices. |

**Knowledge Areas in ACM**

| **Sr.#** | **ACM Knowledge Area** | **Your Project Mapping to ACM Knowledge Area** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1   | AL-Algorithms and Complexity | Stress classification algorithms and feature extraction techniques are used to process physiological sensor data efficiently in real time. |
| 2   | AR-Architecture and Organization | The project uses microcontroller-based hardware architecture integrating sensors, memory, and processing units for embedded AI execution. |
| 3   | CN-Computational Science | Physiological signal processing and numerical analysis are applied to analyze heart rate, GSR, and temperature data. |
| 4   | DS-Discrete Structures | Logical decision-making, state transitions, and classification outcomes are modeled using discrete data representations. |
| 5   | GV-Graphics and Visualization | Stress trends, graphs, and analytics are visualized in the mobile application interface for user interpretation. |
| 6   | HCI-Human-Computer Interaction | User-friendly wearable interaction and mobile app UI are designed to ensure ease of use and effective therapy feedback. |
| 7   | IAS-Information Assurance & Security | User data privacy is ensured through on-device processing and secure Bluetooth communication without cloud dependency. |
| 8   | IM-Information Management | Physiological data collection, storage, and historical stress records are managed efficiently within the system. |
| 9   | IS-Intelligent Systems | TinyML-based machine learning models are used to intelligently detect stress levels and trigger therapy actions. |
| 10  | NC-Networking and Communication | Bluetooth Low Energy communication is used for data exchange between the wearable device and mobile application. |
| 11  | OS-Operating Systems | Embedded firmware scheduling and task management are implemented on the microcontroller environment. |
| 12  | PBD-Platform-based Development | The project is developed on embedded platforms and mobile platforms using Arduino framework and mobile SDKs. |
| 13  | PD-Parallel and Distributed Computing | Sensor data acquisition and processing tasks run concurrently within the embedded system. |

| 14  | PL-Programming Languages | C/C++ is used for embedded development while higher-level languages are used for mobile application development. While Typescript is used for Mobile application. |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 15  | SDF-Software Development Fundamentals | Core software concepts such as modular design, debugging, and testing are applied throughout development. |
| 16  | SE-Software Engineering | System development follows software engineering practices including requirement analysis, design, implementation, and testing. |
| 17  | SF-Systems Fundamentals | The project integrates hardware, software, sensors, communication, and AI into a complete embedded system. |
| 18  | SP-Social Issues and Professional Practice | The project addresses mental health awareness, ethical data usage, and responsible AI practices in healthcare technology. |

#### PBL Feedback by Student

| **Feedback by Student** |
| --- |
| Problem-Based Learning (PBL) is an important tool in our project that helps to locate real-life problems in the context of detecting and managing mental stress. The PBL method stimulates us to examine real-life situations that are encountered by students and professionals who are under stress due to their emotions in real life. Problem analysis helped us to examine constraints on the current mental health app as well as wearable solutions including delayed response, privacy considerations, and absence of direct intervention.<br><br>PBL also helped to choose the right physiological signals, which are heart rate, galvanic skin response, temperature, and movement to detect the stress correctly. It also helped in optimising the TinyML-based machine learning models, to make them able to perform efficient real-time inferences on resource-constrained embedded hardware. Due to the constant performance of the system evaluation, the team enhanced the accuracy of their models, power efficiency, and response time.<br><br>Additionally, PBL was used to design the on-device therapy modules like guided breathing, music therapy, and chatbot interaction which are effective. The strategy was also used to optimize the mobile application interface to make it more usable and user-friendly. In general, the problem-based learning allowed organizing, practical, and solution-focused development process so that NeuroBand can be successfully used to combat real-life mental health issues. |

# Chapter # 1

**Introduction**

##### INTRODUCTION

##### . BACKGROUND

The growing demand for smart healthcare solutions has accelerated research in wearable technology. Wearable devices such as smartwatches and fitness trackers are widely used for monitoring physical parameters like heart rate, activity levels, calories burned, and sleep patterns. However, emotional and psychological health monitoring remains relatively underexplored due to the subjective nature of emotions and the complexity of physiological signals.  
<br/>Recent advancements in biosensor technology have made it possible to capture physiological indicators associated with stress and emotional states. These indicators include heart rate variability (HRV), galvanic skin response (GSR), body temperature, and motion patterns. When combined with machine learning algorithms, these signals can be analyzed to infer stress levels with reasonable accuracy.  
<br/>Simultaneously, the emergence of TinyML has transformed embedded systems by enabling machine learning models to run efficiently on low-power microcontrollers. Unlike cloud-based AI solutions, TinyML allows real-time inference directly on the device, reducing dependency on internet connectivity and enhancing data privacy. NeuroBand leverages these technological advancements to create a self-contained emotional support system capable of detecting stress and initiating therapy without external reliance

##### PROBLEM STATEMENT

Despite the availability of mental health applications and wearable devices, several limitations persist. Most existing solutions depend heavily on smartphones and cloud-based processing, raising concerns related to privacy, latency, and data security. Emotional stress is often detected after it has already intensified, limiting the effectiveness of corrective measures.  
<br/>Furthermore, current systems lack integrated therapy mechanisms. Users are informed about stress levels but are not provided with immediate tools to manage emotional discomfort. There is a clear need for an intelligent, autonomous wearable system capable of continuous monitoring, on-device analysis, and instant therapeutic response.

##### INTRODUCTION

Mental health has become one of the most critical challenges of the twenty-first century, affecting individuals across all age groups, professions, and societies. Rapid technological advancements, academic competition, workplace pressure, financial instability, and lifestyle imbalance have significantly increased stress, anxiety, and emotional disorders. According to global health studies, prolonged stress not only affects mental well-being but also leads to serious physical health problems such as cardiovascular diseases, weakened immunity, sleep disorders, and reduced cognitive performance.  
<br/>Students and working professionals are among the most affected groups, as they are continuously exposed to deadlines, performance evaluations, and social expectations. Unfortunately, traditional mental health support systems are largely reactive, requiring individuals to actively seek professional help after stress levels have already escalated. Social stigma, lack of awareness, high treatment costs, and limited access to mental health professionals further discourage timely intervention.  
<br/>In this context, technology-driven preventive solutions offer a promising alternative. Wearable devices combined with Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Internet of Things (IoT) technologies enable continuous monitoring, early detection, and proactive intervention. NeuroBand is an AI-powered wearable wristband designed to detect emotional stress in real time and provide immediate therapeutic support. The system operates autonomously using embedded machine learning (TinyML), ensuring privacy, low latency, and uninterrupted availability

##### OBJECTIVE OF THE PROJECT

The main aim of this project is to design and develop AI-assisted wearable wristband that will be able to recognize stress in real time and offer therapeutic help to the user instantly. The project plans not to be tied to the traditional stress monitoring solutions but provides a proactive, autonomous, and privacy-sensitive solution that will help not only recognize emotional stress but also help one in dealing with it successfully. The system aims at fostering mental health by embedding intelligence into wearable hardware and monitoring it to intervene in a timely manner.

Integration of various biosensors to measure physiological data related to stress and emotional conditions is one of the important goals of the project. These sensors are heart rate sensors, galvanic skin response (GSR) sensors, temperature sensors and motion sensors. All these indicators are useful information on the physiological status of the user. The project will focus on the need to accurately capture and preprocess this data in real-time so that the project will have reliable stress detection. To develop a powerful system that will be able to be sustained in the real world, it is vital to have a powerful sensor integration.

The other key goal is the adoption of stress classification models based on TinyML on the wearable device. Conventional machine learning systems are typically cloud based, and that results in latency, privacy concerns, and reliance on the internet. This project seeks to eliminate these shortcomings by installing lightweight machine learning models to the microcontroller. With TinyML, inference can happen in real-time and with minimum power consumption and the sensitive user data can be kept on the device. The target is to have correct classification of the stress to be achieved with efficiency, responsiveness and privacy of data.

The project is also interested in creating embedded therapy modules which can offer immediate assistance as soon as they realize that there is some stress. As opposed to the existing systems that merely inform the user of his or her stress level, NeuroBand can help users manage their stress by providing therapeutic functions inbuilt in the system. Such are guided breathing practices based on vibration and light patterns, playing soothing music via the built-in speaker, and the ability to talk to a voice-based chatbot with the help of a rudimentary conversation. The goal is to offer fast and convenient interventions that assist users to manage their emotional level without the need to seek outside help.

Besides the wearable device, there is another critical purpose, called designing and developing a mobile application that can be considered a companion interface of the system. The mobile app will enable the users to visualize the trends in stress levels, examine the past data, control the mode of therapy, and to monitor the overall emotional health. To ensure that there is smooth communication between the wristband and the mobile application, Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) communication will be employed. This goal aims at improving user experience, transparency, and interaction and leave processing at the wearable device.

In general, the project will provide a holistic, intelligent, and user-friendly model of stress detection and management. The system aims at making a contribution to the development of the wearable healthcare technology and proving the usefulness of TinyML in the mental health support systems by integrating biosensors, embedded AI, therapeutic feedback, and user-friendly mobile interface.

##### PROJECT SCOPE

The NeuroBand project scope will entail the entire design, development, and testing of an AI-based wearable device that is capable of detecting and treating stress in real-time. The project is based on a combination of hardware, software and smart algorithms into a small wristband, that can work independently, and a wristband that can emotional support people in real-time. The system is created to illustrate the feasibility of using embedded Artificial Intelligence, Internet of Things (IoT), and human-centered design concepts in the area of mental health technology.

The hardware level entails the design and incorporation of various biosensors that have the capability of recording physiological data of stress and moods. Such sensors are heart rate sensors, galvanic skin response (GSR) sensors, temperature sensors, and motion sensors. This project entails the connection of these sensors to a low-power microcontroller and proper and continuous data collection in the real-life conditions of use. The scope may support proper sensor calibration and signal conditioning and data preprocessing to improve the accuracy of stress detection.

The scope of the project also covers embedded firmware development in control of sensor data collection and system control, as well as, communication between hardware modules. The firmware will handle real-time data processing, power processing, and synchronisation of various modules of the system. The firmware development is also essential to provide low energy usage, responsiveness, and reliability of the wearable device throughout its usage.

The other significant element of the project scope is the implementation of machine learning models on embedded hardware with TinyML methods. The project is aimed at the implementation of lightweight stress classification models, which can be directly inferred without using additional hardware on the microcontroller. This will remove the need to process data in the cloud, decrease the latency, and maintain the privacy of users by storing sensitive physiological data on the device. Important issues in this scope are model optimization, and memory constraints, and performance evaluation.

A Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) communication is also provided in the system to facilitate a smooth interaction between the wearable wristband and a mobile application. Transmission of selected data, synchronization of settings and user interaction are done but low power consumption is also maintained by BLE. The areas include connecting a device, transferring the data securely, and, most importantly, providing a stable connection between the device and the mobile interface.

Also within the scope of the project is the development of a mobile application that will be a companion interface of NeuroBand. The mobile app enables the user to visualize the trends of stress, look at historical data, maintain therapy modes, as well as the overall emotional well-being. The interface of the application is made as user-friendly as possible to increase its accessibility and interest of the user.

The NeuroBand system is designed as a product to be used by the target audience, which is students and working professionals that have daily stress and need a convenient, private, and real-time method of managing emotions. These groups, however, are not the only ones that the scope of the project can cover. The system can be expanded in future to do more things relating to healthcare and wellness, including stress measures on patients, mental health programs at work, and preventive healthcare programs. Therefore, the project scope enables scalability and enhancement in the future as it will support the immediate mental health demands.

##### OUTCOMES OF PROPOSED SYSTEM

The effective usage of the NeuroBand system will provide considerable academic, technical, and practical results in the wearable mental health technology sphere. The system suggested, will offer a proactive and intelligent detection and control of stress based on the integration of embedded Artificial Intelligence, biosensors, and real-time therapeutic intervention in one wearable device. This result will solve the increasing need to find an effective solution to mental health support.

Among the most notable implications of the offered system, the real-time identification of

stress and emotion-based states with the help of physiological indicators is to be mentioned. With the aid of yet embedded machine-learning models that run with TinyML, NeuroBand can conduct inference with a low latency and minimal power consumption. This guarantees that stress is identified in time without the use of cloud infrastructure thus improving user privacy and reliability of the system.

The other significant result is the delivery of direct therapeutic assistance on recognition of stress. In contrast to traditional systems that merely notify users about their stress levels, NeuroBand provides active support to users with an inbuilt therapy system that includes breathing exercises, calming music, and voice chatbot. The interventions allow the user to control their emotional condition in time and minimize the chances of developing stress and related health problems.

The system also encourages self-management and awareness of mental health because the system enables the user to track stress patterns and history of therapy using a companion mobile app. This openness will help users to identify trends in their emotional states and have healthier coping mechanisms.

This project has shown that it is practically feasible to implement TinyML based intelligence into wearable healthcare-based applications, both academically and technologically. The fact that the stress detection models using a resource-constrained microcontroller are successfully implemented is quite good evidence that intelligent systems can effectively work without utilizing cloud computing. The given project is a demonstration of the possibility to use machine learning models with low-power wearable devices and achieve reasonable accuracy, responsiveness, and energy efficiency. On-device inference also solves important issues regarding data privacy and latency, two significant problems in traditional cloud-based medical systems.

In the technical domain, NeuroBand emphasizes how optimized embedded models will be used to help in real time health monitoring without the need to add computational complexity. The system architecture is modular, which means that each of the components

of the system (sensors, machine learning models, therapy modules, communication interfaces, etc.) can be upgraded or replaced. This scalability allows the system to be flexible in the future and extended use.

NeuroBand has the potential of extending its application outside the realms of student and professional stress monitoring into clinical medical care, wellness care, and mental health care in the workplace in the future. The system can be used to assist in the support of preventative healthcare programs, employee welfare programs, and patient monitoring programs. Altogether, the suggested system will lead to the ongoing enhancement of mental healthcare, including innovation, access, and timely interventions based on smart wearable technology.

---

## **HARDWARE ARCHITECTURE**

### **Overview**

The NeuroBand wristband is built upon an ESP32-C3 microcontroller platform, integrating multiple physiological sensors, a display interface, communication modules, and therapeutic components. The hardware architecture is designed to be low-power, compact, and capable of real-time sensor data acquisition while maintaining continuous operation for extended periods.

### **Core Microcontroller**

**ESP32-C3 Microcontroller**
- **Architecture:** RISC-V 32-bit single-core processor
- **Clock Speed:** 160 MHz
- **RAM:** 400 KB SRAM
- **Flash Memory:** 4 MB
- **Power Consumption:** 80-160 mA (with WiFi active), 10-15 mA (idle mode)
- **I2C Support:** Hardware I2C bus for multi-device communication
- **ADC:** 12-bit Analog-to-Digital converters for sensor input
- **GPIO Pins:** 22 available general-purpose I/O pins
- **Wireless:** Built-in BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy) for mobile connectivity
- **Reason for Selection:** Low power consumption, sufficient computational capacity for TinyML inference, integrated BLE, small form factor ideal for wearable applications

### **Physiological Sensors**

#### **1. MAX30102 Heart Rate & SpO2 Sensor**
- **Protocol:** I2C (Address: 0x57)
- **LEDs:** Dual red and infrared LEDs
- **Measurement Type:** Reflective PPG (Photoplethysmography)
- **Sampling Rate:** Configurable up to 1600 Hz (default: 100 Hz)
- **Output:** 18-bit ADC readings for both RED and IR channels
- **Power Consumption:** 50-100 mA (with LEDs active)
- **Application in NeuroBand:** Continuous heart rate monitoring for stress detection; Heart Rate Variability (HRV) analysis to assess autonomic nervous system activity
- **Data Used:** Real-time BPM (beats per minute) and heart rate patterns

#### **2. MPU6050 Accelerometer & Gyroscope**
- **Protocol:** I2C (Address: 0x68)
- **Accelerometer:** 3-axis (X, Y, Z) with ±16g range
- **Gyroscope:** 3-axis angular velocity measurement with ±2000°/s range
- **Sampling Rate:** Configurable up to 8 kHz
- **Power Consumption:** 3-5 mA
- **Application in NeuroBand:** Motion detection, activity level monitoring, fall detection, shake detection for emergency alerts
- **Data Used:** Acceleration magnitude, gyroscopic movement patterns to detect physical activity and abnormal motion events

#### **3. LM35 Temperature Sensor**
- **Protocol:** Analog input (GPIO 4)
- **Temperature Range:** -55°C to +150°C
- **Accuracy:** ±1.75°C (typical)
- **Output:** 10 mV/°C linear voltage output
- **Power Consumption:** 0.06 mA (60 µA)
- **Application in NeuroBand:** Body temperature monitoring; thermal stress indicators
- **Data Used:** Core body temperature as a physiological indicator of stress and emotional arousal

### **Display & User Interface**

#### **SSD1306 OLED Display**
- **Protocol:** I2C (Address: 0x3C)
- **Resolution:** 128 × 64 pixels
- **Color:** Monochrome (white pixels on black background)
- **Refresh Rate:** 60 Hz typical
- **Power Consumption:** 15-20 mA
- **Application in NeuroBand:** Real-time display of heart rate, temperature, stress level, time, and system status
- **Features:** Low power consumption, excellent contrast, suitable for wearable form factor

### **Input Devices**

#### **Panic Button**
- **Type:** Normally-open momentary tactile switch
- **Connection:** GPIO 10 (with internal pull-up resistor)
- **Function:** Emergency alert trigger for immediate therapy or SOS notification
- **Debounce Time:** 50 ms (software debounce in firmware)
- **Application:** User-initiated stress response or emergency alert activation

### **Audio & Haptic Feedback**

#### **Buzzer / Speaker**
- **Type:** Piezoelectric buzzer
- **Connection:** GPIO 5 (digital output with PWM control)
- **Frequency Range:** 2 kHz - 4 kHz (typical)
- **Power Consumption:** 10-30 mA (depending on driving strength)
- **Application in NeuroBand:** 
  - Auditory alerts for stress detection
  - Guided breathing exercise audio cues
  - Therapy intervention notifications
  - Alarm and notification sounds

### **Communication Interface**

#### **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)**
- **Standard:** BLE 5.0 (integrated in ESP32-C3)
- **Range:** Up to 240 meters (line of sight)
- **Data Rate:** 1 Mbps
- **Power Consumption:** 10-50 mA (during active transmission)
- **Protocol:** GATT (Generic Attribute Profile)
- **Application:** Real-time data transmission to companion mobile application
- **Data Transmitted:** Heart rate, temperature, stress classification, therapy status, historical data
- **Security:** Secure pairing with encrypted data communication

### **Power Management**

#### **Battery & Power Supply**
- **Battery Type:** Li-Po (Lithium Polymer) 3.7V nominal
- **Capacity:** 500-1000 mAh (typical for wearable)
- **Estimated Runtime:** 8-12 hours continuous operation (with all sensors active)
- **Charging:** USB-C or proprietary charging dock
- **Power Regulation:** On-board 3.3V voltage regulator from ESP32-C3

#### **Power Distribution**
| Component | Current Draw | Status |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ESP32-C3 (idle) | 10-15 mA | Always-on |
| OLED Display | 15-20 mA | Always-on |
| MAX30102 (active) | 50-100 mA | Periodic/On-demand |
| MPU6050 | 3-5 mA | Always-on |
| LM35 Temperature | 0.06 mA | Always-on |
| Buzzer (active) | 10-30 mA | On alert/therapy |
| **Total (typical)** | **~150-200 mA** | Average operation |

### **Memory & Storage**

#### **Data Storage**
- **Non-Volatile Storage (NVS):** 
  - Time persistence for real-time clock
  - User settings and therapy preferences
  - Calibration data for sensors
- **Flash Memory:** 4 MB total (allocated for firmware and data logging)
- **RAM:** 400 KB for runtime data processing and sensor buffers

### **I2C Bus Configuration**

All I2C sensors share the same bus to minimize GPIO usage and reduce form factor:

| Device | I2C Address | Data Rate |
| --- | --- | --- |
| MAX30102 | 0x57 | 100 kHz |
| MPU6050 | 0x68 | 100 kHz |
| SSD1306 OLED | 0x3C | 100 kHz |

**I2C Bus Pins:**
- **SDA:** GPIO 8 (shared data line)
- **SCL:** GPIO 9 (shared clock line)
- **Clock Frequency:** 100 kHz (optimized for stable multi-device communication)

### **Wiring Summary**

| Component | GPIO/Pin | Function | Voltage |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| MAX30102 SDA | GPIO 8 | I2C Data | 3.3V |
| MAX30102 SCL | GPIO 9 | I2C Clock | 3.3V |
| MPU6050 SDA | GPIO 8 | I2C Data | 3.3V |
| MPU6050 SCL | GPIO 9 | I2C Clock | 3.3V |
| OLED SDA | GPIO 8 | I2C Data | 3.3V |
| OLED SCL | GPIO 9 | I2C Clock | 3.3V |
| LM35 Signal | GPIO 4 | Analog Input | 0-3.3V |
| Panic Button | GPIO 10 | Digital Input | 3.3V (active high) |
| Buzzer | GPIO 5 | Digital Output (PWM) | 3.3V |

### **System Integration**

The hardware components operate in a coordinated manner:

1. **Sensor Acquisition Layer:** MAX30102, MPU6050, and LM35 continuously collect physiological data
2. **Processing Layer:** ESP32-C3 processes raw sensor data and implements TinyML models for stress classification
3. **Display Layer:** OLED screen provides real-time feedback to the user
4. **Communication Layer:** BLE transmits classified stress data and system status to the mobile application
5. **Feedback Layer:** Buzzer delivers audio cues for alerts, alarms, and therapeutic interventions

---

# Chapter # 2

**Literature Review**

- **LITERATURE REVIEW**

Wearable technology has been highly popular over the past few years because of its ability to facilitate constant health tracking and early diagnosis of physical and mental disorders. Smartwatches and fitness trackers are some of the devices that are usually used to track physiological measurements such as heart rate, physical activity, sleep patterns, and calorie expenditure. Although these devices have been shown to be effective in the monitoring of physical health, emotional and psychological health monitoring has not been extensively studied. This is mainly because of subjectivity of emotions and complexity of physiological

signals which are linked to stress and states of minds. Scientists are adopting an increased focus on the fact that stress detection with the help of wearable devices is capable of facilitating proactive mental health care instead of reactive clinical assistance.

The recent developments in biosensor technology have enabled the possibility of capturing physiological pointers that are directly related to stress and emotional reactions. The heart rate variability (HRV) has been extensively researched as a measure of the activity of the autonomic nervous system, and it is usually associated with the degree of stress. Electrodermal activity is also called galvanic skin response (GSR) and it is a variation in the skin conductance due to the stimulation of the sympathetic nervous system and is regarded as one of the best predictors of emotional arousal. Moreover, changes in body temperature and movement data measured by accelerometers and gyroscopes are contextual data that increase the accuracy of stress detection. Research has demonstrated that multimodality stress detectors perform better than single-mentor detectors in the context of stress detection, particularly in more natural situations where motion artifacts and noise are prevalent.

Machine learning methods are important in the analysis of physiological data obtained through wearable sensors. The classical machine learning models like Support Vector Machines, Random Forests, and k-Nearest Neighbors have been extensively used to predict the stress classification and have shown encouraging findings in controlled conditions. More recent studies have discussed deep learning methods of learning multimodal sensor data in complex patterns automatically. Nevertheless, much of these solutions are based on cloud-based processing which creates issues of latency, privacy

and reliance on uninterrupted internet connectivity. Such constraints make stress detection systems impractical to use in real-time and in everyday work.

With the advent of TinyML, the embedded systems have been greatly revolutionized since they can now execute machine learning models on low-power microcontrollers. TinyML can perform on-device inference, minimizing latency, minimizing energy usage to a

minimum, and improving the privacy of information, since sensitive physiological data are stored in device memory. Recent research has shown that machine learning models that are lightweight and deployed with TinyML can be reliably used in health monitoring applications, such as stress detectors, even in the case of limited resources. This strategy is in line with the increasing need of privacy-sensitive and real-time wearable health products.

Published datasets like Wearable Stress and Affect Detection (WESAD) dataset have been widely utilized in the study of stress detection. These data sets contain multimodal physiological data like heart rate, electrodermal activity, temperature and motion data taken under controlled stress and baseline states. The studies conducted on the basis of such datasets point to the efficiency of wrist-based sensors in detecting stress but also demonstrate the difficulties in terms of generalization between users and settings. The findings highlight the significance of an effective feature extraction, sensor fusion, and model optimization to be used in the real world.

Besides the detection of stress, recent literature also emphasizes the role of therapeutic interventions in digital mental health systems. Digital mental health intervention studies have indicated that guided breathing exercises and relaxation music, as well as conversational support, can be effective in alleviating stress and enhancing emotional well-being, especially when in a convenient and timely fashion. But, the majority of available systems offer therapy via mobile apps, but not directly via wearables. This division of detection and intervention inhibits the timeliness and efficiency of dealing with stress.

Research on technology-assisted mental health solutions is still scarce in the context of Pakistan. The majority of the current literature is dedicated to awareness campaigns, mobile health applications, or survey-based mental health assessment. It is evident that there is a research gap on autonomous

wearable systems, which integrate stress detection with real-time therapeutic intervention. This deficiency shows the necessity of new wearable solutions that are affordable, privacy-conscious, and locally-appropriate.

In general, the literature suggests that wearable stress detection and analysis of physiological signals has made a lot of progress but there are still a number of gaps. These are reliance on cloud computing, insufficient integration of therapeutic processes, difficulties in real-life resiliency, and the absence of locally-worn mental health. The NeuroBand project bridges these gaps as it involves the implementation of multi-sensor stress detection, TinyML-based on-device inference, and real-time therapeutic assistance into a single wearable device, which is the contribution to intelligent, real-time, and user-centered mental health technologies

.**2.1 RESEARCH PAPER SUMMARIES**

**2.1.1 Detection of Stress with the help of Wearable Physiological and Sociometric Sensors.**

The paper examines the concept of stress detection using physiological along with sociometric information gathered using wearable technology. The authors believe that stress cannot be explained by the physiological response only but also by the social interactions and the context. The physiological data like the heart rate and other attributes are recorded together with the sociometric data like the distance to other people and communication mannerism. The proposed study will combine the two sources of data together, which will enhance the effectiveness of real-time stress detection.

Several machine learning classifiers are tested to determine the stress discrimination capabilities of these classifiers in controlled experimental conditions. The results indicate that the performance of multimodal models integrating physiological and sociometric data is always better than the one of the models using a single source of information. This stresses the fact that context-related sensing is critical in stress recognition. The study proves that the use of physiological measures might not be a reliable measure of the level of stress, particularly in social dynamic situations.

The research is especially applicable to the wearable stress detection system, because it

highlights the

importance of combining more than one data modality. In this case of NeuroBand, it can be argued that the combination of multiple physiological sensors increases the robustness and accuracy of a project according to this research. Even though sociometric sensing is not a direct aspect of NeuroBand, the research supports the idea of multimodal stress sensing and identifies the drawbacks of using a single signal. All in all, the study presents a useful contribution to the real-time stress detection and supports the idea of multi-sensor solutions in wearable mental health systems.

**2.1.2 Deep neural network in Stress Detection.**

The study explores the application of deep learning models into detecting stress and emotion based on wearable sensors. The researchers suggest the deep neural network architectures, such as One-dimensional Convolutional Neural Networks (1D-CNNs) and Multilayer Perceptrons (MLPs), which can be used to process the physiological time-series data measured by wearable devices. The evaluation of the effectiveness of these models is done in comparison with the traditional machine learning methods.

The experiment shows that deep learning models are much better than traditional classifiers in continuous and non-invasive stress detection exercises. Among the key points of benefits, the fact that deep neural networks can automatically extract the relevant features out of raw sensor data is mentioned, eliminating the necessity of hand-crafting the features. This comes in handy especially in complicated physiological signals where manual features might not be adequate to capture subtle patterns of stress.

The conclusions support the fact that deep learning can be effectively used in wearable-based monitoring of stress, particularly when it comes to time-series data of impressive volume. In the case of the NeuroBand project, this paper gives a solid reason as to why intelligent models can be utilized in the analysis of physiological signals. Although NeuroBand is more of a TinyML deployment than a heavy deep network, the study is

demonstrating the wider fact that more advanced learning models yield more accurate recognition of stress. It also points out the trade-off between model complexity and deployability, which is a major factor to consider in embedded wearable systems.

**2.1.3 Deep Neural Networks to classify Mental Stress with Image-Encoding.**

This paper presents a new method of stress classification, which transforms physiological time-series measurements into image formations and then uses deep neural networks. The image-encoding algorithms like Gramian Angular Fields (GAF) transform signals like ECG and accelerator signals. Deep learning models are then fed with these images, which are then used to classify stress.

The suggested approach results in a very high accuracy in classification on benchmark sets of stresses which indicates that sophisticated techniques of signal transformations work. The model transforms the temporal data into the visual patterns and the relations that might be difficult to detect using other conventional feature-based techniques are captured. This study has pointed out the possibilities of using signal processing combined with deep learning to detect stress better.

The computational costs of the approach are rather high, but the study can inform people about the value of other feature representation strategies. In the case of NeuroBand, which is defined upon embedded and resource-constrained hardware, it might not be possible to directly implement such models. Nevertheless, the study adds to the insight on ways to better represent physiological data in order to classify it. It also supports the fact that there should be innovative methods of enhancing the accuracy of stress detection in wearable devices.

**2.1.4** **Machine Learning Model to Recognize Emotions using Physiological Cues.**

This study dwells on the possibility of detecting emotions based on such physiological

measures as galvanic skin response and other similar aspects. The authors categorize the emotional conditions such as happiness, sadness, and neutral mood by applying machine learning methods. Support Vector Machines (SVMs) are among the evaluated models that are good at classification of emotions.

The work demonstrates that physiological indicators can be successful in reflecting emotional conditions and that a basic machine learning algorithm can be used to deliver reliable outcomes in case the right features are chosen. The mentioned finding is especially significant to wearable systems, in which the computational resources are constrained. The study indicates that the use of complex

models is not the only way to produce satisfactory performance in emotion recognition.

In the case of the project of the NeuroBand, the research serves as an excellent background to adopt the application of physiological signs (GSR) in detecting stress, and emotions. It also confirms that it is possible to run lightweight models on embedded devices. The study supports the concept that effective and properly structured models may provide significant outcomes without a large amount of computational baggage, which is consistent with the TinyML-based strategy of the project.

**2.1.5 Wearable Stress Monitoring Generalizable Machine Learning.**

This systematic review offers an in-depth review of machine learning methods in wearable stress monitoring. The authors analyze various datasets, widely-used physiological attributes including HRV and EDA, and a vast variety of machine learning methods. Another topic of the review is the experiment protocols and assessment measures of the studies.

This review has a major contribution on the particular issue of model generalization. The authors point out that most stress detecting models are effective in laboratory environments with controlled conditions but fail to generalize to new users, environments and stressors.

Lack of uniform labeling, small sample size and variability at inter-individual levels are cited as critical weaknesses in current studies.

The review is very much applicable in the case of NeuroBand because it provides grounds on why effective, real world stress detection systems are required. It advocates multimodal sensing and cautious model design so as to enhance generalization. Privacy-saving and real-time solutions are also considered significant in the study and this supports the rationale of embedded and on-device stress detection systems.

**2.1.6 ReLearn: Multimodal Stress Detection by Robust Machine Learning Framework.**

The ReLearn model is suggested to counteract the problem of physiological data gaps or gaps in wearable stress sensors. Common in the real-world wearable deployments include sensor malfunctions, noises, and data loss. This paper presents a machine learning architecture that can continue working with a certain level of performance in the event that certain sensor inputs are missing.

The framework has a high accuracy of stress classification in different missing-data cases. This renders it especially appropriate in practice when it is impossible to guarantee the perfect availability of data. The study underscores the value of strength and resilience of wearable stress trackers.

In case of NeuroBand, this research can be a great insight in terms of getting an idea of the actual deployment issues. It helps to strengthen the necessity of robust system design and multimodal sensing. Although the stress detection system designed in NeuroBand might not directly apply the ReLearn framework, the study confirms the need to design stress detection systems capable of working with imperfect conditions of data.

**2.1.7 Mental Health Assessment with Novel sensors Wearable Device Dataset.**

This paper proposes a massive data on mental health measurement with the help of a sophisticated sensory technology Laser Doppler Flowmetry (LDF) and Fluorescence Spectroscopy (FS). The data is to be used in predicting stress and mental health through machine learning. As it was experimentally demonstrated, the results of machine learning models are promising on the suggested dataset.

The research has a contribution to the field by increasing the scope of sensors in wearable mental health research. It shows that new sensors have the capacity to record useful physiological data in addition to the conventional heart rate and GSR measurements. This study identifies the future prospects of the wearable stress detection systems.

Despite the fact that NeuroBand concentrates on more accessible sensors, the given research offers valuable information about the new trends in wearable sensing. It promotes the scalability and

extensibility of wearable mental health systems in the long term and is a motivator to investigate new sensor technologies in the future**.**

**2.1.8 Microcontroller EdgeML EdgeML of Stress and Sleep.**

The study explores the use of machine learning models on the microcontrollers in stress and sleep detection with the use of heart rate variability data. The paper shows that it is possible to integrate lightweight models based on the application of TinyML on resource-limited devices.

The findings are that embedded models are reliable in performance and also consume low power and responsive in real time. This study justifies the design decisions undertaken in NeuroBand, especially the application of TinyML to classify stress on-devices. It confirms

the feasibility of built-in AI in wearable healthcare tools.

Other techniques of optimization that the study addresses include model compression and feature extraction that are critical to wearable deployment. Comprehensively, this study offers a high technical rationale of applying stress detection models on microcontrollers.

**2.1.9 Machine Learning HRV-Based Models in the Real-Time Detection of Stress.**

The proposed research aims at real-time stress detection based on HRV features produced by wearable devices. The authors compare several machine learning models and prove that HRV-based stress models can be conducted successfully in real time.

The study highlights the value of early detection of stress to preventive health measures. It demonstrates that wearable systems can constantly track the stress, but not to interrupt the work of a user. These results are highly corroborating the aim of NeuroBand, which consists of detecting stress with real-time accuracy and intervening in it immediately.

The present paper confirms the choice of HRV as a fundamental physiological measure of wearable stress monitoring technology and justifies the possibility of real-time implementation**.**

**2.2 Researches in Pakistan**

Mental health is a developing community health issue in Pakistan, and the emotional well-being is strongly influenced by such factors as academic pressure, economic instability, social expectations, and access to professional mental health care. Although the problem of stress, anxiety, and depression is getting more and more common, the research and technological intervention associated with mental health is not as common as in developed countries. The research on the topic in Pakistan considers the majority of existing studies to be based on the awareness, screening, and evaluation instead of actual detection and intervention in the context of intelligent wearable devices.

There are a number of researches done in Pakistan, which highlight the extent of stress among students in universities. In academic studies, the publications in local and international journals indicate that academic workload, examination pressure, future career uncertainty, and social expectations are some of the main contributors to the psychological stress donning students. These researches are mainly based on self-reported surveys or stress scales and questionnaires to determine mental health conditions. Although these methods offer a good understanding of how stress is prevalent, they are not capable of real-time monitoring as well as being extremely reliant on subjective responses, which do not necessarily always indicate the actual level of stress in an individual.

Mobile health (mHealth) applications are a recent development that have shown interest in Pakistan as a possible remedy to mental health awareness and support. A number of research studies and scholarly programs have investigated the use of smartphone applications, which deliver mental health education, stress management information and self-assessment options. Mood tracking, relaxation techniques, and mental health questionnaires are some of the applications that are usually included in these applications. These solutions are better in terms of accessibility and awareness, but still, they require user intervention and do not offer continuous and automatic stress detection. Furthermore, the solutions involving smartphone are restrained by user interaction, internet addiction, and late response.

The studies concerning wearable health technology in Pakistan have mainly been dealing with physical health monitoring, including monitoring the heart rate, fitness tracking, and fundamental biomedical

signal acquisition. Few studies have investigated the application of wearable sensors that are of low cost to monitor cardiovascular or physical activity levels. Nevertheless, wearable devices in mental health, especially the stress and emotion detection, has not been discovered thoroughly. The majority of the wearable-related studies do not include the incorporation with smart algorithms and prioritize the hardware prototype over the real-time data analysis and interpretation.

The development of machine learning and artificial intelligence in Pakistan has been on the rise in the last ten years particularly in fields like image processing, speech recognition, medical diagnosis, and data analytics. A number of scholarly publications prove that machine learning methods are effective in healthcare applications, such as disease prediction and patient monitoring. Nevertheless, machine learning methods in analyzing mental health based on physiological data are yet to be developed. Current literature is generally based on offline datasets and lacks discussion of real-time implementation on embedded or wearable systems.

Research has also been done in Pakistan to detect stress by physiological factors like heart rate and electrodermal activity under controlled conditions. These studies usually entail the gathering of sensor data of study participants and using simple machine learning classifiers to determine stress patterns. Although these studies present encouraging outcomes, they are primarily research works that are not practically applied in wearable equipment. Furthermore, these system types tend to use desktop-based processing or cloud-analytical services, which constrain their use in real-time, sustained load monitoring.

The other limitation that is also early noted on the Pakistani research is the absence of the consideration on privacy preserving and offline capable systems. The majority of technology-based mental health solutions rely on cloud type processing and connection to the internet, thus posing questions of data security and privacy of the user. Considering the sensitivity of the information about mental health and the different levels of digital trust of users in Pakistan, there is a dire need of solutions that are capable of working independently without the constant flow of data. Nevertheless, the number of local studies which discuss this aspect is very low, which indicates a research gap.

Therapeutically, studies in Pakistan mostly focus on counseling, psychotherapies, as well as clinical

interventions that are provided using conventional healthcare facilities. Guided breathing,

relaxation, and conversations agents are the common examples of digital therapy solutions that are not often incorporated in technological systems. In the case of digital interventions, they are not interactive or adaptive but rather informational. It shows that there is no research on stress detection and instant therapeutic reaction, especially by wearable or embedded computing.

Pakistan In embedded systems and TinyML, the field of research is immature. The majority of embedded AI projects are aimed at smart home automation, energy management, or simple IoT projects. Healthcare monitoring on a microcontroller through the implementation of machine learning models is a topic that is scarcely addressed in local studies. Consequently, the commitment to academic contribution on low-power, real-time, and smart wearable health devices that are not dependent on cloud infrastructure is insufficient.

In general, the analysis of the current studies in Pakistan shows that there are some severe gaps. To begin with, the majority of research in the field of mental health is not based on objective physiological measurements but on a subjective evaluation. Second, wearable technology studies are more concerned with the supervision of physical health, but not emotional or psychological well-being. Third, applications of machine learning in mental health are largely offline and do not have real-time embedded development. Lastly, minimal detection and therapy has been integrated into a single system.

The project of NeuroBand will directly fill these gaps by introducing an AI-based wearable wristband as a device that integrates physiological detection, on-device stress detection based on TinyML, and timely therapeutic intervention. The project corresponds to the unmet needs in the Pakistani research by concentrating on autonomy, privacy, and real-time operation. Moreover, NeuroBand also leads to the local academic and technological progress since it indicates that it is

possible to implement intelligent wearable mental health systems in the Pakistani environment, and thus, the given project is a worthy contribution to the current scope of research**.**

**2.3 Literature Analysis:**

The review of the existing literature on stress detection, emotion recognition, and wearable healthcare systems indicates that it is a fast-developing area in research, which incorporates physiological measurements, machine learning, and digital health intervention. Although there has been one great advance in the detection of stress using wearable machines, other limitations to real-time applicability, system independence, privacy, robustness, and therapeutic incorporation are also observed in the literature. This section is a critical analysis of the reviewed studies in order to draw the trends, strengths, weaknesses, and gaps in the research that makes the proposed NeuroBand system necessary.

One of the most notable tendencies in literature is that the use of physiological cues to detect stress and emotions is becoming increasingly prevalent. The variables that are reliably reported as indicators of stress are heart rate variability (HRV), galvanic skin response (GSR), skin temperature and motion data. Research indicates that HRV is an indicator of the activity of the autonomic nervous system, whereas GSR is an indicator of sympathetic excitement related to emotional stress. Temperature and movement data can serve as auxiliary signals to help give context and minimize noise due to physical activity. The validity of using multi-sensor physiological data to detect stress is reinforced by the fact that the results of the previous studies are consistent and it would be a direct justification to the sensor selection strategy of NeuroBand.

The literature does however show that single sensor techniques cannot be used to effectively detect stress especially in the real world. Most of the initial works demonstrated promising accuracy when tested in controlled laboratory environments but failed when applied in daily-life environments. Signal quality is highly impacted by motion artifacts and sensor noise as well as personal variability. Consequently, the recent studies focus more on the multimodal sensing, which involves the integration of various physiological and environmental indications to enhance reliability. This movement towards multimodal

constructs justifies the NeuroBand model of utilizing more than one biosensor as opposed to a single physiological parameter.

Machine learning is a key aspect in the classification of stress throughout the literature. Normal machine learning models including Support Vector Machines, Random Forests and k-Nearest Neighbors are commonly applied because of their interpretability and the relatively low cost in

computation. A number of studies prove that these models can provide sensible accuracy when properly integrated features are produced out of physiological signals. Nevertheless, the literature also points out that the traditional models, in most cases, involve a lot of manual feature engineering that restricts scalability and flexibility to various users and datasets.

Conversely, deep learning methods have become popular because they can automatically extract complicated patterns out of raw sensor data. Research on convolutional neural networks and multilayer perceptrons has continued to indicate that these models provide better classification results than conventional techniques. The use of more advanced methods like image encoding of time-series data also contributes to more accurate models because it helps to capture the temporal relationships in physiological signals. Although performance of deep learning models has been excellent, their new features come with new challenges such as high level of computation, consumes more energy, and cannot be easily deployed on the resource limited wearable devices.

This is a critical issue in the literature which is identified as a trade-off between performance and deployability. Although deep learning models are good in offline or cloud scenarios, most wearable stress detection systems are based on cloud computing, which creates a latency risk, privacy issues, and reliance on the internet connection. A number of the studies recognize these weaknesses and do not go further to offer viable solutions. The development of TinyML and EdgeML is an important change in this respect. Embedded machine learning studies have shown that small models can be executed directly on a microcontroller with

real-time inference through light power consumption and enhanced privacy.

The relevance of health monitoring with TinyML-based stress detection based on HRV and other physiological parameters is proved by literature on this topic. These studies point to optimization methods which include model compression, quantization and efficient feature extraction as a way of operating models with memory limits of microcontrollers. There are however, limited studies that use TinyML to detect mental health and stresses. This shows that there is a very evident research opportunity, which NeuroBand is trying to fill by using TinyML-based stress classification in a wearable wristband.

One more important point that has been found in the literature is the data generalization and strength. The systematic reviews consistently highlight the fact that a lot of stress detection models cannot be generalized to different population groups, environments, and stressors. Lack of external validity is caused by small sample sizes, inconsistent labeling protocols, and controlled experimental designs. Moreover, sensor data can be lost or lost or corrupted by hardware constraints or human resources, which is common to real-world wearable implementations. Strong structures capable of processing incomplete data are as yet quite uncommon, but recent studies suggest ways of addressing these issues.

One of the most important gaps in the available literature is the problem of integration of interventions and therapies. A high percentage of wearable stress detection devices are only interested in stress level monitoring and reporting but not the action support. Although the digital mental health interventions (guided breathing, relaxation audio, and chatbot-based support) have been examined independently, there is a lack of direct integration with wearable devices. The vast majority of therapeutic interventions are provided using mobile apps, and this distance between detecting stress and responding to it is formed. Such a distance makes stress management less effective and immediate particularly when dealing with situations that demand quick intervention.

The NeuroBand project attempts to fill this gap by incorporating the therapy mechanisms directly into the wearable device. The digital interventions literature allows concluding

about the efficacy of guided breathing and relaxation methods in the process of stress reduction, especially in those cases when they take place at the appropriate time. Nonetheless, the deficiency of real-time, automatic intervention in the current systems underscores the originality of the integration of the stress-detecting and treatment functionality into one autonomous wearable device.

The issue of privacy and data security also features high in the literature. Mental health information is extremely confidential and cloud-based processing presents justifiable fears of misuse of information and the trust of the users. A number of studies recognise the dangers of privacy but fail to give any tangible architectural fixes. TinyML on-device inference becomes a powerful reaction to these issues, as it reduces the costs of data transmission as well as maintains sensitive data on the device. The design of NeuroBand is consistent with this direction of privacy preservation found in literature.

In geographical and contextual terms, the literature review shows that there was a major gap in the researches by the developing nations especially Pakistan. Although wearable stress detection studies are dominated by international studies, local studies in Pakistan mainly feature survey-based stress assessment, awareness campaigns and mobile health applications. Little work is done regarding autonomous wearable mental health systems and there is even less literature on embedded AI or TinyML. The absence of local research restricts the externalization of global solutions to the local environment, cultural standards, and infrastructure limitations.

Another weakness of the reviewed literature is that the user experience and long-term adoption are not explored extensively. There are numerous studies that consider technical performance measures like accuracy and precision and fail to consider usability, comfort, and user engagement. Wearable gadgets that are supposed to be used all the time should balance both technical and comfort features, accessibility, and convenience. The transparency and involvement of the user with a visualization and control option of the neuroband through the presence of a companion mobile application eliminates this weakness as it improves this feature which has been underrepresented in most of the current literature.

Overall, the literature review showed that the wearable stress detection and physiological signal analysis have come a long way, but there are some significant gaps still. These are dependence on cloud computing, inability to implement in real-time, and not robust enough in real-world environments, inability to integrate therapeutic interventions, and a shortage of studies in the Pakistani environment. These gaps are the strongest reasons to support the NeuroBand project, as it suggests a comprehensive approach to solving the problem, which implies combining multimodal sensing, the TinyML-based on-device inference, instant therapy, and user-focused design into a

single wearable solution. Using both technical and contextual constraints as highlighted in the literature, NeuroBand will make an important contribution to the future of intelligent, real-time, and privacy-conscious mental health technologies

**References**

\[1\] M. Gjoreski, H. Gjoreski, M. Luštrek, and M. Gams, "Stress detection using wearable physiological and sociometric sensors," _IEEE J. Biomed. Health Inform._, vol. 20, no. 6, pp. 1546-1556, Nov. 2016.

\[2\] S. J. Hossain, M. G. Mostafa, and S. S. Islam, "Stress detection using deep neural networks," _BMC Med. Inform. Decis. Mak._, vol. 20, no. 1, pp. 1-14, Oct. 2020.

\[3\] A. Al-Makhadmeh and A. Tolba, "Classification of mental stress from wearable physiological sensors using image-encoding-based deep neural network," _Sensors_, vol. 22, no. 24, pp. 1-18, Dec. 2022.

\[4\] A. Subramanian, J. Wache, M. Kessler, and F. Schwenker, "A machine learning model for emotion recognition from physiological signals," _Biomed. Signal Process. Control_, vol. 51, pp. 1-9, May 2019.

\[5\] R. Can, C. Ersoy, and M. G. Bayram, "Generalizable machine learning for stress monitoring from wearable devices," _arXiv preprint arXiv:2209.15137_, 2022.

\[6\] Y. Gao, S. Li, and Y. Wang, "ReLearn: Robust machine learning framework for multimodal stress detection," _arXiv preprint arXiv:2104.14278_, 2021.

\[7\] M. A. Khan et al., "Wearable device dataset for mental health assessment using LDF and FS sensors," _arXiv preprint arXiv:2502.00973_, 2025.

\[8\] M. P. Ríos, J. Torres, and L. M. Vargas, "Microcontroller-based EdgeML: Health monitoring for stress and sleep via HRV," _Eng. Proc._, vol. 78, no. 1, pp. 1-6, 2024.

\[9\] A. Javed, S. Rehman, and M. Iqbal, "Real-time stress detection in wearable devices using HRV-based machine learning models," _Int. J. Eng. Technol._, vol. 9, no. 3, pp. 45-52, 2024.

\[10\] M. A. Sano and R. W. Picard, "Stress recognition using wearable sensors and mobile phones," _IEEE Trans. Affect. Comput._, vol. 8, no. 3, pp. 1-14, Jul.-Sep. 2017.

\[11\] J. A. Healey and R. W. Picard, "Detecting stress during real-world driving tasks using physiological sensors," _IEEE Trans. Intell. Transp. Syst._, vol. 6, no. 2, pp. 156-166, Jun. 2005.

\[12\] M. Schmidt, "Identifying stress states using wearable sensors," in _Proc. IEEE Int. Conf. Affective Comput. Intell. Interact._, Sep. 2018, pp. 1-6.

\[13\] D. Lane et al., "BeWell: A smartphone application to monitor, model and promote wellbeing," _IEEE Pervasive Comput._, vol. 10, no. 4, pp. 1-9, Oct.-Dec. 2011.

\[14\] Z. Zhang, J. Gao, and W. Li, "TinyML-based wearable health monitoring systems: A survey," _IEEE Access_, vol. 10, pp. 112345-112360, 2022.

\[15\] P. Kumar and U. Singh, "Wearable IoT based stress monitoring using physiological parameters," _Int. J. Med. Eng. Inform._, vol. 12, no. 2, pp. 95-107, 2020.

\[16\] S. Banerjee and R. Gupta, "Emotion recognition using physiological signals: A review," _IEEE Rev. Biomed. Eng._, vol. 14, pp. 1-15, 2021.

\[17\] Y. LeCun, Y. Bengio, and G. Hinton, "Deep learning," _Nature_, vol. 521, no. 7553, pp. 436-444, May 2015.

\[18\] A. M. Rashid, H. Ali, and S. Ahmed, "Machine learning approaches for mental health monitoring: A review," _J. Med. Syst._, vol. 45, no. 3, pp. 1-14, 2021.

\[19\] TensorFlow, "TensorFlow Lite for Microcontrollers," TensorFlow Documentation, 2023. \[Online\]. Available: <https://www.tensorflow.org/lite/microcontrollers>
